verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Paul also and Barnabas continued in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, with many others also.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe again their humility, how they let others also take part in the preaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And some days after Paul said unto Barnabas, Let us go again and visit our brethren in every city where we have preached the word of the Lord, and see how they do.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Barnabas determined to take with them John, whose surname was Mark.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But Paul thought not good (ἡξίου see note 3, p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 213) to take him with them, who departed from them from Pamphylia, and went not with them to the work.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the contention (or exasperation) was so sharp between them, that they departed asunder one from the other.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And already indeed Luke has described to us the character of the Apostles, that the one was more tender and indulgent, but this one more strict and austere.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the gifts are diverse—(the gifts, I say), for that this is a gift is manifest—but the one befitting one, the other another set of characters, and if they change places, harm results instead of good. (b) In the Prophets too we find this: diverse minds, diverse characters: for instance, Elias austere, Moses meek.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So here Paul is more vehement.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe for all this, how gentle he is.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Thought not good,” it says, “to take him with them that had departed from them from Pamphylia.” (a) And there seems indeed to be exasperation (παροξυσμός), but in fact the whole matter is a plan of the Divine Providence, that each should receive his proper place: and it behooved that they should not be upon a par, but the one should lead, and the other be led.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And so Barnabas took Mark, and sailed unto Cyprus; and Paul chose Silas, and departed, being recommended by the brethren unto the grace of God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the Churches.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this also is a work of Providence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the Cyprians had exhibited nothing of the like sort as they at Antioch and the rest: and those needed the softer character, but these needed such a character as Paul’s.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Which then,” say you, “did well? he that took, or he that left?” *** (c) For just as a general would not choose to have a low person always to his baggage-bearer, so neither did the Apostle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This corrected the other’s, and instructed (Mark) himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then did Barnabas ill?” say you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And how is it not amiss (ἄτοπον), that upon so small a matter there should arise so great an evil?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In the first place then, no evil did come of it, if, sufficing each for whole nations, they were divided the one from the other, but a great good.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, they would not readily have chosen to leave each other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But admire, I pray you, the writer, how he does not conceal this either.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But at any rate,” say you, “if they must needs part, let it be without exasperation.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, but if nothing more, observe this, that in this too is shown what was of man (in the preaching of the Gospel).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if the like behooved to be shown (even) in what Christ did, much more here.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, the contention cannot be said to be evil, when each disputes for such objects (as here) and with just reason.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I grant you, if the exasperation were in seeking his own, and contending for his own honor, this might well be (reproved): but if wishing, both the one and the other, to instruct and teach, the one took this way and the other that, what is there to find fault with?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in many things they acted upon their human judgment; for they were not stocks or stones.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And observe how Paul impeaches (Mark), and gives the reason.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of his exceeding humility he reverenced Barnabas, as having been partner with him in so great works, and being with him: but still he did not so reverence him, as to overlook (what was necessary).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now which of them advised best, it is not for us to pronounce: but thus far (we may affirm), that it was a great arrangement of Providence, if these were to be vouchsafed a second visitation, but those were not to be visited even once.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (a) “Teaching and preaching the word of the Lord.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They did not simply tarry in Antioch, but taught.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What did they “teach,” and what “preach” (evangelize)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They both (taught) those that were already believers, and (evangelized) those that were not yet such.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And some days after,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For because there were offences without number, their presence was needed. (d) “How they do,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this he did not know: naturally.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See him ever alert, solicitous, not bearing to sit idle, though he underwent dangers without end.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark, it was not of cowardice that he came to Antioch?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He acts just as a physician does in the case of the sick.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the need of visiting them he showed by saying, “In which we preached the word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Barnabas determined,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (So) Barnabas “departed, and went not with (him).” (b) The point to be considered, is not that they differed in their opinions, but that they accommodated themselves the one to the other (seeing), that thus it was a greater good their being parted: and the matter took a pretext from this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What then? did they withdraw in enmity?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In fact you see after this Barnabas receiving many encomiums from Paul in the Epistles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There was “sharp contention,” it says, not enmity nor quarrelling.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The contention availed so far as to part them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And Barnabas took Mark,” etc. And with reason: for what each supposed to be profitable, he did not forego thereafter, because of the fellowship with the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, it seems to me that the parting took place advisedly (κατὰ σύνεσιν), and that they said one to another, “As I wish not, and thou wishest, therefore that we may not fight, let us distribute the places.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that in fact they did this, altogether yielding each to the other: for Barnabas wished Paul’s plan to stand, therefore withdrew; on the other hand, Paul wished the other’s plan to stand, therefore he withdrew.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Would to God we too made such separations, as to go forth for preaching.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A wonderful man this is; and exceedingly great!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To Mark this contest was exceedingly beneficial.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the awe inspired by Paul converted him, while the kindness of Barnabas caused that he was not left behind: so that they contend indeed, but the gain comes to one and the same end.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed, seeing Paul choosing to leave him, he would be exceedingly awed, and would condemn himself, and seeing Barnabas so taking his part, he would love him exceedingly: and so the disciple was corrected by the contention of the teachers: so far was he from being offended thereby.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if indeed they did this with a view to their own honor, he might well be offended: but if for his salvation, and they contend for one and the same object, to show that he who honored him * * * had well determined, what is there amiss (ἄτοπον) in it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (e) “But Paul,” it says, “departed, having chosen Silas, and being commended to the grace of God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They prayed it says: they besought God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See on all occasions how the prayer of the brethren can do great things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now he journeyed by land, wishing even by his journeying to benefit those who saw (τοὺς ὁρὥντας) him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when indeed they were in haste they sailed, but now not so. (c) “And he went through Syria and Cilicia, confirming the Churches.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then came he to Derbe and Lystra.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the wisdom of Paul: he does not go to other cities before he has visited them which had received the Word.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is folly to run at random.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This let us also do: let us teach the first in the first place, that these may not become an hindrance to them that are to come after.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And, behold a certain disciple was there, named Timotheus, the son of a certain woman, which was a Jewess, and believed; but his father was a Greek: which was well reported of by the brethren that were at Lystra and Iconium.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Him would Paul have to go forth with him; and took and circumcised him because of the Jews which were in those quarters; for they knew all that his father was a Greek.” (ch. xvi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is indeed amazing, the wisdom of Paul!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He that has had so many battles about circumcision, he that moved all things to this end, and did not give over until he had carried his point, now that the decree is made sure, circumcises the disciple.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He not only does not forbid others, but himself does this thing. (b) “Him,” it says, “he would have to go forth with him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the wonder is this, that he even took him unto him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Because of the Jews,” it says, “which were in those parts:” for they would not endure to hear the word from one uncircumcised. (a) Nothing could be wiser.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that in all things he looked to what was profitable: he did nothing upon his own preference (προλήψει). (c) And what (then)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark the success: he circumcised, that he might take away circumcision: for he preached the decrees of the Apostles.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And as they went through the cities, they delivered them the decrees for to keep, that were ordained of the Apostles and elders which were at Jerusalem.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And so were the Churches established in the faith, and increased in number daily.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Dost thou mark fighting, and by fighting, edification?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not warred upon by others, but themselves doing contrary things, so they edified the Church!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They introduced a decree not to circumcise, and he circumcises!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And so were the Churches,” it says, “established in the faith,” and in multitude: “increased,” it says, “in number daily.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then he does not continue to tarry with these, as having come to visit them: but how? he goes further.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Now when they had gone throughout Phrygia and the region of Galatia, and were forbidden of the Holy Ghost to preach the word in Asia,” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6.) having left Phrygia and Galatia, they hastened into the interior.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For, it says, “After they were come to Mysia, they assayed to go into Bithynia: but the Spirit suffered them not.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore they were forbidden, he does not say, but that they were “forbidden,” he does say, teaching us to obey and not ask questions, and showing that they did many things as men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the Spirit,” it says, “suffered them not: but having passed by Mysia they came down to Troas.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a vision appeared to Paul in the night; There stood a man of Macedonia, and prayed him, saying, Come over into Macedonia, and help us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why a vision, and not the Holy Ghost? because He forbade the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He would even in this way draw them over: since to the saints also He appeared in a dream, and in the beginning (Paul) himself saw a vision, “a man coming in and laying his hands upon him.” (ch. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: In this manner also Christ appears to him, saying, “Thou must stand before Cæsar.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then for this reason also He draws him thither, that the preaching may be extended.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why he was forbidden to tarry long in the other cities, Christ urging him on.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For these were to enjoy the benefit of John for a long time, and perhaps did not extremely need him (Paul), but thither he behooved to go.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And now he crosses over and goes forth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after he had seen the vision, immediately we endeavored to go into Macedonia, assuredly gathering that the Lord had called us for to preach the Gospel unto them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then the writer mentions also the places, as relating a history, and showing where he made a stay (namely), in the greater cities, but passed by the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore loosing from Troas, we came with a straight course to Samothracia, and the next day to Neapolis; and from thence to Philippi, which is the chief city of that part of Macedonia, and a colony.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a high distinction for a city, the being a colony.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And in this city we were tarrying certain days.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us look over again what has been said.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after some days, Paul said,” etc. (ch. xv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He put to Barnabas a necessity for their going abroad, saying “Let us visit the cities in which we preached the word.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But Paul begged,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet no need for him to beg, who had to make an accusation presently.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This happens even in the case where God and men are the parties: the man requests, God is wroth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For instance, when He saith, “If her father had spit in her face” (Num. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14): and again, “Let me alone, and in Mine anger I will blot out this people.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And Samuel when he mourns for Saul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For by both, great good is done.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also here: the one is wroth, the other not so.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same happens also in matters where we are concerned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the sharp contention with good reason, that Mark may receive a lesson, and the affair may not seem mere stage-playing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is not to be thought that he who bids, “Let not the sun go down upon your wrath,” (Eph. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26) would have been wroth because of such a matter as this: nor that he who on all occasions gave way would not have given way here, he who so greatly loved Paul that before this he sought him in Tarsus, and brought him to the Apostles, and undertook the alms in common with him, and in common the business relating to the decree.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But they take themselves so as to instruct and make perfect by their separation them that need the teaching which was to come from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he rebukes others indeed, but bids do good to all men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As in fact he does elsewhere, saying, “But ye, be not weary in well-doing.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Thess. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This we also do in our common practice.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Here it seems to me that others also were alike displeased with Paul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And thereupon taking them also apart, he does all, and exhorts and admonishes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Much can concord do, much can charity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Though it be for a great matter thou askest; though thou be unworthy, thou shalt be heard for thy purpose of heart: fear not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “He went,” it says, “through” the cities “And, behold, there was a disciple, by name Timothy, who had a good report of the brethren which were in Lystra and Iconium.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Great was the grace of Timothy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When Barnabas departed (ἀπέστη), he finds another, equivalent to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of him he saith, “Remembering thy tears and thy unfeigned faith, which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and in thy mother Eunice.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: His father continued to be a Gentile, and therefore it was that (Timothy) was not circumcised. (a) Observe the Law already broken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Or if not so, I suppose he was born after the preaching of the Gospel but this is perhaps not so. (c) He was about to make him a bishop, and it was not meet that he should be uncircumcised. (e) And this was not a small matter, seeing it offended after so long a time: (b) “for from a child,” he says, “thou hast known the Holy Scriptures.” (ib. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15.) (d) “And as they went through the cities, they delivered them the decrees for to keep.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For until then, there was no need for the Gentiles to keep any such.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The beginning of the abrogation was the Gentiles’ not keeping these things, and being none the worse for it: nor having any inferiority in respect of faith: anon, of their own will they abandoned the Law. (f) Since therefore he was about to preach, that he might not smite the Jews a double blow, he circumcised Timothy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet he was but half (a Jew by birth), his father being a Greek: but yet, because that was a great point carried in the cause of the Gentiles, he did not care for this: for the Word must needs be disseminated: therefore also he with his own hands circumcised him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And so were the churches established in the faith.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark here also how from going counter (to his own object) a great good results?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And increased in number daily.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you observe, that the circumcising not only did no harm, but was even of the greatest service?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And a vision appeared unto Paul in the night.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not now by Angels, as to Philip, as to Cornellius, but how?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By a vision it is now shown to him: in more human sort, not now as before (i.e., v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 6, 7) in more divine manner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For where the compliance is more easy, it is done in more human sort; but where great force was needed, there in more divine.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For since he was but urged to preach, to this end it is shown him in a dream: but to forbear preaching, he could not readily endure: to this end the Holy Ghost reveals it to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also it was then with Peter, “Arise, go down.” (ch. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For of course the Holy Spirit did not work what was otherwise easy: but (here) even a dream sufficed him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And to Joseph also, as being readily moved to compliance, the appearance is in a dream, but to the rest in waking vision.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus to Cornelius, and to Paul himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And lo, a man of Macedonia,” etc. and not simply enjoining, but “beseeching,” and from the very persons in need of (spiritual) cure. (ch. x.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Assuredly gathering,” it says, “that the Lord had called us.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10), that is, inferring, both from the circumstance that Paul saw it and none other, and from the having been “forbidden by the Spirit,” and from their being on the borders; from all these they gathered.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore loosing from Troas, we came with a straight course,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That is, even the voyage made this manifest: for there was no tardiness.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It became the very root of Macedonia.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was not always in the way of “sharp contention” that the Holy Spirit wrought: but this so rapid progress (of the Word) was a token that the thing was more than human.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet it is not said that Barnabas was exasperated, but, “Between them there arose a sharp contention.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If the one was not exasperated neither was the other.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Knowing this, let us not merely pick out (ἐκλέγωμεν) these things, but let us learn and be taught by them: for they were not written without a purpose.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a great evil to be ignorant of the Scriptures: from the things we ought to get good from, we get evil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus also medicines of healing virtue, often, from the ignorance of those who use them, ruin and destroy: and arms which are meant to protect, are themselves the cause of death unless one know how to put them on.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the reason is, that we seek everything rather than what is good for ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in the case of a house, we seek what is good for it, and we would not endure to see it decaying with age, or tottering, or hurt by storms: but for our soul we make no account: nay, even should we see its foundations rotting, or the fabric and the roof, we make no account of it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, if we possess brute creatures, we seek what is good for them: we call in both horse-feeders and horse-doctors, and all besides: we attend to their housing, and charge those who are entrusted with them, that they may not drive them at random or carelessly, nor take them out by night at unseasonable hours nor sell away their provender; and there are many laws laid down by us for the good of the brute creatures: but for that of our soul there is no account taken.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But why speak I of brute creatures which are useful to us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are many who keep small birds (or “sparrows”) which are useful for nothing except that they simply amuse, and there are many laws even about them, and nothing is neglected or without order, and we take care for everything rather than for our own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus we make our selves more worthless than all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if indeed a person abusively call us “dog,” we are annoyed: but while we are opprobrious to ourselves, not in word, but in deed, and do not even bestow as much care on our soul as on dogs, we think it no great harm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see how all is full of darkness?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How many are careful about their dogs, that they may not be filled with more than the proper food, that so they may be keen and fit for hunting, being set on by famine and hunger: but for themselves they have no care to avoid luxury: and the brute creatures indeed they teach to exercise philosophy, while they let themselves sink down into the savageness of the brutes.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The thing is a riddle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And where are your philosophic brutes?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are such; or, say, do you not take it to be philosophy, when a dog gnawed with hunger, after having hunted and caught his prey, abstains from the food; and though he sees his meal ready before him, and with hunger urging him on, yet waits for his master?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Be ashamed of yourselves: teach your bellies to be as philosophic.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You have no excuse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When you have been able to implant such philosophic self-command in an irrational nature, which neither speaks nor hears reason, shall you not much more be able to implant it in yourself?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that it is the effect of man’s care, not of nature is plain: since otherwise all dogs ought to have this habit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you then become as dogs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it is you that compel me to fetch my examples thence: for indeed they should be drawn from heavenly things; but since if I speak of those, you say, “Those are (too) great,” therefore I speak nothing of heavenly things: again, if I speak of Paul, you say, “He was an Apostle:” therefore neither do I mention Paul: if again I speak of a man, you say, “That person could do it:” therefore I do not mention a man even, but a brute creature; a creature too, that has not this habit by nature, lest you should say that it effected this by nature, and not (which is the fact) from choice: and what is wonderful, choice not self-acquired, but (the result of) your care.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The creature does not give a thought to the fatigue, the wear and tear it has undergone in running down the prey, not a thought to this, that by its own proper toil it has made the capture: but casting away all these regards, it observes the command of its master, and shows itself superior to the cravings of appetite.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “True; because it looks to be praised, it looks to get a greater meal.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Say then to yourself, that the dog through hope of future pleasure, despises that which is present: while you do not choose for hope of future good things to despise those which are present; but he indeed knows, that, if he tastes of that food at the wrong time and against his master’s will, he will both be deprived of that, and not get even that which was apportioned to him, but receive blows instead of food: whereas you cannot even perceive this, and that which he has learnt by dint of custom, you do not succeed in acquiring even from reason.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us imitate the dogs.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The same thing hawks also and eagles are said to do: what the dogs do with regard to hares and deer, the same do those with regard to birds; and these too act from a philosophy learnt from men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These facts are enough to condemn us, these enough to convict us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To mention another thing:—they that are skilled in breaking horses, shall take them, wild, fierce, kicking, biting, and in a short time so discipline them, that though the teacher be not there, it is a luxury to ride them, their paces are so thoroughly well-ordered: but the paces of the soul may be all disordered, and none cares for it: it bounds, and kicks, and its rider is dragged along the ground like a child, and makes a most disgraceful figure, and yet no one puts curbs on her, and leg-ties, and bits, nor mounts upon her the skilful rider—Christ, I mean.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And therefore it is that all is turned upside down.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when you both teach dogs to master the craving of the belly, and tame the fury in a lion, and the unruliness of horses, and teach the birds to speak plainly, how inconsistent must it not be—to implant achievements of reason in natures that are without reason, and to import the passions of creatures without reason into natures endowed with reason?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There is no excuse for us, none.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: All who have succeeded (in mastering their passions) will accuse us, both believers and unbelievers: for even unbelievers have so succeeded; yea, and wild beasts, and dogs, not men only: and we shall accuse our own selves, since we succeed, when we will, but when we are slothful, we are dragged away.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For indeed many even of those who live a very wicked life, have oftentimes changed themselves when they wished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But the cause is, as I said, that we go about seeking for what is good for other things, not what is good for ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If you build a splendid house, you know what is good for the house, not what is good for yourself: if you take a beautiful garment, you know what is good for the body, not for yourself: and if you get a good horse, it is so likewise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: None makes it his mark how his soul shall be beautiful; and yet, when that is beautiful, there is no need of any of those things: as, if that be not beautiful, there is no good of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For like as in the case of a bride, though there be chambers hung with tapestry wrought with gold, though there be choirs of the fairest and most beautiful women, though there be roses and garlands, though there be a comely bridegroom, and the maidservants and female friends, and everybody about them be handsome, yet, if the bride herself be full of deformity, there is no good of all those; as on the other hand if she were beautiful, neither would there be any loss arising from (the want of) those, nay just the contrary; for in the case of an ugly bride, those would make her look all the uglier, while in the other case, the beautiful would look all the more beautiful: just so, the soul, when she is beautiful, not only needs none of those adjuncts, but they even cast a shade over her beauty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For we shall see the philosopher shine, not so much when in wealth, as in poverty.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For in the former case many will impute it to his riches, that he is not superior to riches: but when he lives with poverty for his mate, and shines through all, and will not let himself be compelled to do anything base, then none claims shares with him in the crown of philosophy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us then make our soul beauteous, if at least we would fain be rich.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What profit is it, when your mules indeed are white and plump and in good condition, but you who are drawn by them are lean and scurvy and ill-favored?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What is the gain, when your carpets indeed are soft and beautiful, full of rich embroidery and art, and your soul goes clad in rags, or even naked and foul?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What the gain, when the horse indeed has his paces beautifully ordered, more like dancing than stepping, while the rider, together with his choral train and adorned with more than bridal ornaments, is more crooked than the lame, and has no more command over hands and feet than drunkards and madmen?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Tell me now, if some one were to give you a beautiful horse, and to distort your body, what would be the profit?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now you have your soul distorted, and care you not for it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us at length, I beseech you, have a care for our own selves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do not let us make our own selves more worthless than all beside.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If anyone insult us with words, we are annoyed and vexed: but insulting ourselves as we do by our deeds, we do not give a thought to it.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us, though late, come at last to our senses, that we may be enabled by having much care for our soul, and laying hold upon virtue, to obtain eternal good things, through the grace and mercy of our Lord Jesus Christ, with Whom to the Father, together with the Holy Spirit, be glory, might, honor, now and evermore, world without end.
